title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a conversation with Jimmy, Lady Cutt expresses her sorrow regarding Sir Oswald's plan to give their home to a company, noting that his taste is low and he is driven by an insatiable desire for more wealth and status. Mrs. Cutt mentions that Sir Oswald places great trust in the judgment of Mr. Bettman, a diligent worker at his factory. Jimmy recalls a pleasant weekend at the Chimney House, though he notes the sadness of Gerry's death during that time. Mrs. Cutt asks Jimmy if he is interested in a girl, specifically mentioning Vera Daventry, whom she refers to by the nickname "the socks." Jimmy expresses a desire to see Vera again, and Mrs. Cutt invites him to their house the following weekend when she is visiting.

After Jimmy leaves, Sir Oswald expresses his dislike for the boy, calling him a troublemaker. Lady Cutt informs Oswald that she has already invited Mr. Cecil to visit the following weekend. Oswald objects, revealing that Mr. Bettman warned him to be careful of Cecil, as they were former classmates. Although Lady Cutt expresses regret for the misunderstanding, she continues her knitting with a subtle smile.

In a separate setting, Lord Kettlem discusses golf with Bondel. Lord Kettlem has taken up the game with great enthusiasm but poor skill, frequently tearing up the turf. McTavish attempts to repair the damage caused by Lord Kettlem's shots. Bondel suggests that McTavish's frustration with the Cutlers is finally being met with "just desserts." Bunty remarks that golf keeps Lord Kettlem out of trouble, while Lord Kettlem claims to have achieved a hole-in-one. Bunty also observes that while Osborne Cutt's golf technique is stiff and accurate, he is unable to make short putts, causing him to lose games.
